overview: 4c GenericBlink is a tempo strategy that leverages bounce effects to repeatedly trigger enter-the-battlefield abilities and disrupt opponent boards. By cycling through Quantum Riddler, Overlord of the Balemurk, and Phlage, the deck maintains board presence while applying relentless pressure across the W/U/B/R color pie. The four-color mana base supports flexible spellcasting but requires careful sequencing to avoid mana screw in Modern.
win_conditions: Trading up with bounce effects to maintain board advantage
Overwhelming opponents with repeated ETB triggers from signature creatures
Closing out games with efficient removal and board wipes

keep_criteria: Hands with two or more lands and a signature creature
Hands containing early bounce spells or removal
Hands with mana dorks or ramp to smooth the four-color base
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for four-color), at least one instant-speed blink enabler, and a one- or two-mana creature.
mulligan_criteria: Hands with three or more lands
Hands lacking bounce effects or signature creatures
Hands with only high-cost finishers and no early plays
Mulligan if you have three or more non-basic lands without fetch/shock support, multiple lands with no spells, or only high-cost threats with no interaction or blink spells.

early_game: Play lands and low-cost creatures to establish board presence
Use early bounce spells to disrupt opponent's early threats
Prioritize mana development to support the four-color base
Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a one-mana cantrip or early threat.
Turn 2: Cast a blink spell on your creature or a cheap threat to trigger ETB, then follow up with another spell.
Prioritize establishing board presence with value-generating creatures while holding up interaction for key threats.
Sequence mana to ensure you can blink on the opponent's turn if needed.
mid_game: Begin cycling signature creatures to trigger ETB abilities repeatedly
Trade opponent's threats for your own using bounce effects
Maintain card advantage by bouncing creatures instead of destroying them
Preserve mana for instant-speed interaction and blink spells.
Avoid overcommitting to the board without blink enablers ready.
Use the graveyard strategically if the deck includes recursion, but remain vigilant against hate cards.
Commit threats only when you have a plan to blink them or protect them.
If the opponent has removal, hold back high-value targets until you can blink them into safety or answer their interaction.
Use bounce effects to temporarily remove aggressive threats.
late_game: Focus on closing the game with efficient combat tricks and removal
Use board wipes selectively to clear the way for your signature creatures
Leverage mana advantage to cast multiple spells per turn
Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury is the primary win condition, often deployed after clearing the board or when the opponent has no interaction.
Quantum Riddler and Overlord of the Balemurk provide secondary win conditions through repeated value and board swings.
Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no interaction, or you can safely blink Phlage into a board-clearing ETB.
Commit when you've established card advantage and can out-value their remaining resources.

key_cards.name: Quantum Riddler
key_cards.role: Primary bounce engine and card advantage source
key_cards.notes: Use to bounce opponent's threats or your own creatures to trigger ETB abilities; prioritize bouncing high-value targets.

key_cards.name: Overlord of the Balemurk
key_cards.notes: Deploy to clear opponent's board while bouncing creatures back to their hands; sequence after establishing your own board presence.

key_cards.name: Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury
key_cards.notes: Hold until you have sufficient mana and board presence; use its ETB trigger to clear the board and establish dominance.

sequencing_tips: Always bounce your own creatures before bouncing opponent's threats to maximize ETB triggers
Prioritize mana development in the early game to avoid being stuck with uncastable spells
Use removal spells to handle threats that cannot be bounced, preserving bounce effects for recurring value
Sequence board wipes after bouncing your own creatures to avoid losing them
Blink effects resolve as a single spell, making them hard to counter with single-target counters.
Be mindful of 'enter the battlefield' vs 'leaves the battlefield' triggers.
Don't blink creatures with negative ETB effects.
Coordinate your mana carefully due to the four-color requirement
fetch lands and shock lands are critical.
Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without a blink enabler ready, leaving you vulnerable to removal or board wipes.

matchups.advice: Prioritize early bounce effects to delay their attacks and use removal for key threats
Play around counterspells and board wipes by holding up mana and bouncing your own creatures first
Race them by cycling signature creatures and using bounce effects to maintain board presence

Use multiple low-cost spells to force counter windows, employ instant-speed interaction to clear the way, and rely on blink effects that resolve as a single spell or use alternative costs. Chain triggers to out-value their counters.
Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development, use bounce/blink to return their ramp artifacts/creatures, and prioritize fast threats that can close the game before they stabilize.

Win by out-valuing through better card draw, more efficient blink enablers, and superior sequencing. Prioritize protecting your value engines and disrupting their blink triggers with targeted removal or counters.

common_threats: Watch for counterspells, targeted removal, and board wipes.
Note when opponents save removal for specific threats.
Track graveyard activity if facing combo or recursion decks, and monitor mana development to anticipate their next play.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: Archetype name and signature cards imply a bounce/tempo strategy in Modern
